current_fact.fact_text: In 1995, astronaut Norman Thagard quietly made history by becoming the first American to reach space aboard a Russian launch vehicle, riding a Soyuz rocket to the Mir space station. His mission marked a symbolic pivot from Cold War rivalry to orbital collaboration, showing how former adversaries could share a cramped spacecraft in the name of science.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: A literal 'destination space' story pairs with an episode themed as a voyage, featuring cosmic-leaning tracks like Pink Floyd’s “Breathe” and trip‑hop atmospheres.

queue.fact_text: Back in 1663, Otto von Guericke completed his book *Experimenta Nova*, where he described eerie demonstrations with brass hemispheres that could not be pulled apart once the air was pumped out. His vacuum experiments and the first clear description of electrostatic repulsion helped humans realize that 'empty' space is anything but simple.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

queue.match_reason: Guericke’s work on vacuums literally redefined physical emptiness, mirroring the episode’s idea of 'emptiness as space to create' and its energizing, expansive sound.

queue.fact_text: When *The Mikado* premiered in London in 1885, its satirical vision of a fictionalized Japan was so catchy that some audiences whistled its tunes in the streets after the first performance. The opera skewered British society under the mask of an exotic setting, using playful melodies to hide razor‑sharp social commentary.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Both the opera and the episode explore culturally rich performance traditions that travel the world, blending theatrical music with deeper commentary on society.

queue.fact_text: On March 14, 1945, the RAF dropped the 'Grand Slam' bomb—an enormous 10‑ton 'earthquake bomb'—on a railway viaduct near Bielefeld, Germany. Rather than just blast the structure, it was designed to shake the ground itself, collapsing targets with shockwaves rippling through the Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A massive 'earthquake bomb' delivering shockwaves through the ground fits with an 'Electric Shock' set full of explosive riffs and high‑energy guitar.

queue.fact_text: In 1961, a U.S. Air Force B‑52 bomber carrying nuclear weapons crashed near Yuba City, California, after structural failure tore the aircraft apart in mid‑air. The bombs did not detonate, but the accident became a chilling reminder of how close routine flights sometimes came to catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Both the crash and the episode examine hidden risks in powerful technologies—nuclear weapons or always‑on networks—and the uneasy balance between convenience and danger.

queue.fact_text: Pelican Island became the United States' first national wildlife refuge in 1903, when President Theodore Roosevelt set it aside to protect brown pelicans from being wiped out for their feathers. From a tiny mangrove island in Florida grew a whole system of refuges dedicated to giving wild species safe places to simply exist.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A landmark act in ocean and bird conservation resonates with an episode set among whales and Arctic seas, both celebrating the protection and wonder of wild animals.

queue.fact_text: In 2019, Cyclone Idai slammed into Mozambique near the city of Beira, driving seawater and torrential rain far inland. The storm created a vast, temporary inland sea visible from space and became one of the deadliest cyclones ever recorded in the Southern Hemisphere.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description: In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

queue.match_reason: A devastating cyclone and an episode explicitly about rain and weathering storms connect thematically, with the music offering a hopeful counterpoint to the destructive power of water.

queue.fact_text: The 1988 Johnson South Reef Skirmish saw Chinese and Vietnamese forces clash over a submerged reef in the Spratly Islands, a place more coral than land. In a few violent minutes, competing maps and maritime claims were enforced with gunfire above turquoise waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A tense naval skirmish over reefs contrasts with an episode about peaceful voyages at sea, linking through the shared setting of open water and contested maritime spaces.

queue.fact_text: When *Alam Ara* was released in 1931, audiences in India crowded around a single speaker placed under the cinema screen, amazed that the actors were suddenly singing and speaking. As the country's first talking film, it transformed movie‑going into a musical, multilingual experience overnight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description: In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: The first Indian sound film and the rise of Auto‑Tune both mark technological turning points in how voices are heard on recordings and screens, reshaping global popular music.

queue.fact_text: Rossini’s *Petite messe solennelle* had its first performance in 1864 not in a grand cathedral but in a private Parisian mansion, with just twelve singers, two pianos, and a harmonium. The composer jokingly called it his 'little solemn mass' and wondered whether he was writing 'sacred music or damned music,' blending reverence with theatrical flair.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: A chamber‑sized sacred work played in an intimate home resonates with an episode of small‑scale, intricate jazz meant for quiet, reflective spaces rather than grand stages.

queue.fact_text: In 1780, during the American Revolutionary War, Spanish forces captured Fort Charlotte in Mobile, Alabama, cutting off the last British outpost capable of threatening New Orleans from the east. The battle showed how the war’s map stretched far beyond the thirteen colonies, pulling Gulf Coast and colonial empires into the struggle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A multi‑empire battle along shifting colonial borders pairs with an episode about travel and changing perspectives, both tracing how movement across regions reshapes history and identity.

queue.fact_text: In 2017, Japan held a formal naming ceremony for the synthetic element nihonium, with then Crown Prince Naruhito attending to honor the discovery by Japanese scientists. The element’s name, meaning 'the land of the rising sun,' marked the first time an element on the periodic table was named after an Asian country.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A new element named 'nihonium' in Tokyo parallels the Japanese aesthetic of wabi‑sabi, with both celebrating national identity and subtle shifts in how we see matter and imperfection.

queue.fact_text: In 2008, protests and riots erupted in Lhasa and spread across Tibet, combining long‑standing grievances over religion, culture, and autonomy into a brief, highly visible flash of unrest. Images of monks, smoke, and soldiers in the 'roof of the world' traveled globally, underscoring how remote regions can become sudden focal points of world attention.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: Both the Tibetan unrest and Tuareg desert music emerge from high‑altitude or desert regions where culture, land, and resistance are tightly linked and expressed through powerful, atmospheric soundscapes.

queue.fact_text: When the Escondida mine was officially inaugurated in Chile’s Atacama Desert in 1991, it was just the beginning of what would become the world’s most productive copper mine. The operation digs metal from one of the driest places on Earth, turning an ancient desert plateau into a linchpin of the global electrical age.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A desert super‑mine feeding the world’s copper wires mirrors trip‑hop’s wired, urban atmospheres—both products of the late 20th century’s deep reliance on electronics and hidden infrastructure.

queue.fact_text: In 1647, amid the grinding chaos of the Thirty Years' War, Bavaria, Cologne, France, and Sweden signed the Truce of Ulm. It was a fragile pause in one of Europe’s most devastating conflicts, a moment when exhausted powers briefly chose negotiation over cannon fire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A war‑weary truce functions like a collective deep breath, matching an episode built around slowing down, calming rhythms, and taking a pause from intensity.

queue.fact_text: In 1980, LOT Polish Airlines Flight 007 crashed near Warsaw on final approach, killing all 87 on board, including a 14‑member American boxing team bound for an international tournament. The tragedy erased a generation of athletes in a single moment, a loss felt in both Poland and the boxing world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: A major aviation tragedy near Warsaw connects directly with an episode centered on traveling through Poland, bringing a somber historical layer to the same landscapes and cities.

queue.fact_text: Sterling Airways Flight 296 struck a mountainside near Kalba in the United Arab Emirates in 1972 while approaching Dubai, killing 112 people. The crash highlighted how navigation errors and limited terrain awareness could turn an ordinary descent into a collision with unseen desert hills.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A dramatic end to a journey into Dubai’s growing hub contrasts with an episode about safe urban arrivals and city wandering, both framed around international airports and long‑distance travel.

queue.fact_text: In 1964, nightclub owner Jack Ruby was convicted for the on‑camera killing of Lee Harvey Oswald, the accused assassin of President John F. Kennedy. The case welded together nightclub culture, live television, and national trauma into one of the most unsettling legal spectacles in modern history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: The Ruby–Oswald saga sits at the intersection of violence, media, and power—territory Rage Against The Machine explore sonically and lyrically in explicitly political tracks.

queue.fact_text: In 1951, United Nations forces recaptured Seoul for the second time during the Korean War, in a city that would change hands four times over the course of the conflict. The zigzagging control of the capital turned its streets into a map of Cold War tension drawn in ruins and rebuilding.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: A war over who gets to shape a capital city’s future parallels an episode amplifying voices of resistance and empowerment in hip‑hop, especially Queen Latifah’s "U.N.I.T.Y." about standing firm in hostile environments.

queue.fact_text: In 1942, Anne Miller became the first American patient successfully treated with penicillin, recovering from a severe, septic infection that had nearly killed her. Her survival turned a moldy laboratory curiosity into a life‑saving wonder drug, opening the antibiotic era.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Penicillin’s breakthrough came after years of trial, error, and tinkering—just like the slow, iterative learning process explored in the episode, where confusion eventually leads to life‑changing progress.

queue.fact_text: Norman Thagard’s 1995 mission wasn’t the only notable March 14 space story: his flight also marked the start of a continuous American presence on the Russian Mir station that lasted more than two years. It was a rehearsal for the kind of long‑term, multi‑national cooperation that would later define the International Space Station.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: The 'solar wind' and international orbit of Mir link nicely with an episode that opens on ambient cosmic textures ('Solar Wind, Pt. 3') and travels across borders in sound.

queue.fact_text: Another ripple of March 14 in the skies came with the evolution of long‑range bombers like the B‑52 that crashed near Yuba City, which were originally designed to deliver nuclear weapons across continents. These aircraft effectively turned the entire planet into a potential battlefield, forcing humanity to grapple with the consequences of global‑scale technology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: The global reach of strategic bombers and the 'cosmic' language of machines both reflect how mid‑20th‑century technology extended human influence across the planet and into the skies, here flipped into a more playful, dancefloor context.

queue.fact_text: On March 14, 1903, Roosevelt’s protection of Pelican Island created a precedent that would eventually expand to over 560 wildlife refuges in the United States. What began as a defense of birds from hat‑makers’ feathers helped inspire a broader idea: that entire landscapes could be preserved for their own sake.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: A seminal conservation act and an episode about waking up among trees and roots reggae both honor the healing power of nature and spaces left undisturbed by development.

queue.fact_text: By the time Anne Miller received penicillin in 1942, the drug had been discovered more than a decade earlier but resisted mass production. Her recovery showed that this elusive mold extract could reliably pull humans back from the edge, catalyzing the race to scale up fermentation and save millions of lives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A story of medical recovery and the dawn of antibiotics aligns with an episode about healing from injury, both tracing slow journeys from vulnerability back toward strength.

published.fact_text: In 1995, astronaut Norman Thagard quietly made history by becoming the first American to reach space aboard a Russian launch vehicle, riding a Soyuz rocket to the Mir space station. His mission marked a symbolic pivot from Cold War rivalry to orbital collaboration, showing how former adversaries could share a cramped spacecraft in the name of science.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)
